source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: System Assignment Principles
CONTENT:
Systems (e.g. IT products) must be assigned only at the initiative level, not redundantly at the objective level: • This avoids data duplication and reduces maintenance complexity. • Assignments should be made at the lowest meaningful level, typically the IT product. Best Practice: Avoid assigning the same system to both the Initiative and the Objective.
subject: Assignment

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Specific Modelling Rules and Best Practices
CONTENT:
Important: No two IT Products in LeanIX can have the same name and version. If a name conflict occurs: • Check if a new version should be created. • Otherwise, assign a new, unique name. Versioning • Versioning is managed via the "Release" attribute.
subject: IT_Product

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Responsible / Subscriptions
CONTENT:
Each Objective Fact Sheet requires a person in the subscriptions who is responsible for the content and quality of the data. Currently, this person can have the role of Product Owner or Business Owner. The assignment of type, person and role is done via the subscriptions in the respective fact sheet. The owner organization and the assigned person are solely responsible for the creation and maintenance of an objective fact sheet in terms of both
subject: Objective_Fact_Sheet

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Technology Architecture (TOGAF® D)
CONTENT:
• IT Component (Color: Dark Blue) • Vendor (Color: Teal) • Tech Capability (Color: Light Green) Describes the technical infrastructure and supporting technology components.
subject: Technology_Architecture

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Modeling Guidelines
CONTENT:
A Digital Solution must follow the naming convention defined by the ART that owns it. This ensures consistency and traceability across the portfolio. Each Digital Solution must include at least one assigned IT Product. These IT Products represent the technical building blocks that deliver business functionality and are developed and maintained by agile teams within the ART.
subject: Consistency

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Best Practices
CONTENT:
• Use Parent/Child relations to group related Fact Sheets under a common context.
• Ensure that the hierarchy reflects actual ownership or architectural structure.
• Avoid circular or ambiguous relationships.
• Document the rationale for the hierarchy in the description field of the Fact Sheet if needed.
• If the Parent/Child relation is not applicable, explicitly set the field to “Leave empty” to ensure clarity and data quality.
subject: Fact_Sheet

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits
CONTENT:
• A Digital Solution delivers end-to-end value to internal or external customers.
• Customers do not purchase isolated features or capabilities—they seek complete solutions that fulfill their desired outcomes.
• Digital Solutions enable organizations to align technical delivery with business strategy and customer needs.
subject: Organization

SECTION_TITLE: Zusätzliche Relationen
CONTENT:
In addition to the automatically existing assignments between IT product and process, which are synchronized from BIC, business capabilities and modeling initiatives can be assigned to a process. Manual assignment of IT products via LeanIX should be avoided and should be done exclusively in BIC! Further Information Detailed technical and functional information about the synchronization between LeanIX and BIC can be found in the chapter LeanIX System

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Technical Implementation of Interfaces
CONTENT:
Interfaces are not always implemented directly by the IT Product that provides or consumes them. In many cases, middleware platforms, API gateways, or integration services handle the technical implementation. This separation between logical ownership and technical realization is essential for accurate modeling and architecture governance. Modeling Guidelines: • The logical interface is modeled between the provider and consumer IT Pro
subject: Logical_Interface

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Architectural Objects and Attributes
CONTENT:
Each object in LeanIX is represented by a dedicated Fact Sheet. These are interconnected through mappings to form a comprehensive, domain-oriented, and technical architecture. The attributes of each object are based on the LeanIX standard data model and are extended with Porsche-specific attributes, especially in the areas of: • Security • Data Protection • Governance Each Fact Sheet type includes mandatory fields required to meet PASS
subject: Fact_Sheet

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Business Capability Impact
CONTENT:
• Link each Objective to the affected business capabilities.
• Use existing capabilities where possible; define new ones only if necessary.
• Ensure the impact is traceable and aligned with the capability model.
subject: Objective

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits
CONTENT:
From an enterprise architecture perspective, the IT Product is the central modeling object across all architecture layers. It enables: • Functional development planning • Portfolio and release planning • Centralized documentation of: o Security o Compliance o Data protection o Governance requirements The IT Product is the primary reference object for ensuring: • Compliance with the Federal Data Protection Act (BDSG) • Fulfillment of corporate due dili
subject: Centralized_Documentation

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Subtypes in LeanIX
CONTENT:
In LeanIX, business capabilities are divided into two subtypes: Structured Element • Used to organize and group business capabilities hierarchically. • Cannot be linked directly to other fact sheets. • Typically represents Level 1 or Level 2 capabilities. Business Capability • Used to describe actual functional abilities in detail.
subject: Structured_Element

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits of Objectives
CONTENT:
• Identify critical capabilities that are essential for achieving strategic goals.
• Gain organization-wide transparency into how well objectives are supported across business domains.
• Detect gaps or misalignments between strategic intent and operational execution.
subject: Objective

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Objective
CONTENT:
An Objective Fact Sheet is used to represent a strategic goal and the transformations required to achieve it. It serves as a formalized anchor for planning, tracking, and aligning enterprise-wide initiatives with business strategy. Objectives provide structure and traceability for transformation programs and enable consistent communication across business and IT stakeholders. Summary
Summary
subject: Objective

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Link to Objectives
CONTENT:
• The Objective provides strategic context and justification.
• Initiatives without a corresponding Objective are considered invalid from an architectural perspective.
• The relationship between Initiative and Objective ensures alignment and traceability.
subject: Initiative

CONTENT:

• Use the Portfolio Fact Sheet when responsibility is anchored in a business or IT portfolio.
• This ensures clear accountability, supports agile governance, and aligns with LeanIX modeling standards.

CONTENT:
This metamodel provides a structured view of how digital solutions are defined and contextualized within enterprise architecture. It supports traceability from strategic initiatives to technical implementation and organizational responsibility, facilitating alignment between SAFe delivery structures and TOGAF® architectural principles.
subject: Metamodel

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits of Business Process
CONTENT:
Modeling Strategic and Operational Insight Business process modeling is a key element of the business architecture. It helps identify: • Processes that are strategically or economically critical, • Processes that are under pressure to change due to transformation, regulation, or inefficiencies. This insight supports prioritization and investment decisions across business and IT.
subject: Insight
